opts: The ref made a bad call on the change — the refs often miss line changes at this level and this one clearly shouldn't have been blown down at all
Too many men on the ice — the fresh player jumped on before the tired player was close enough to the bench. The outgoing player needs to be within arm's reach of the bench.
Offside at center — the fresh player jumped onto the ice past the red line while the puck was still in the defensive zone, so the play was ruled offside
Icing on the tired player — the tired player skated past center without playing the puck so the ref blew it down for icing against the team that was changing
Give up the goal and reset — once you're down you can't recover, so accept it and get back in position for the next shift after the puck drop
Throw your stick or extend whatever body part you can reach into the path of the puck — any contact can change its direction
Call for a penalty on the shooter — wave your glove at the ref because you were knocked down and ask for a delayed call on the play
Stay still and trust the post — the puck usually hits the pipe from that angle, so freezing in place is the best chance to get lucky
You dump the puck deep and chase — a flat-footed D won't react fast, but dumping the puck in is always the safer play than carrying it across the line
You use your speed to carry the puck wide around the flat-footed defender — your speed advantage makes the carry the best option
You stop at the blue line and wait — let the defender commit first, then make your read once your wingers catch up for a full zone entry
You pass it backward to the trailer — drop the puck to your center at center ice and let them attack with fresh speed through the neutral zone
They should chase even harder — when you're down a man you need to pressure the puck all over the zone to force a bad pass from their power play
They're breaking the PK structure — on the penalty kill, stay in your box and force them to make plays around you instead of chasing
They should go to the bench to change — a tired PK player chasing around is worse than no player, so they need to get off the ice right away
They need to ice the puck more often to kill time — any chance to dump it down the ice is a good one when you're killing a penalty off
You hold the puck forever on your skate — keeping it pinned kills time, tires the defender, and eventually the ref will blow the whistle for a stoppage in play
You kick the puck to your stick and make a quick pass to your linemate before the next defender arrives — you won the battle, now use it
You leave the puck and skate away — once you've tied up the defender your job is done, so release to the slot and let a teammate grab it
You shoot the puck on net right off your skate — any puck toward the net from the boards can cause a rebound that a teammate can tap in quick
Nothing — just bad puck luck, the rebound bounced to a bad spot and the shooter happened to be standing right there when it came out of your pads
Your rebound went to the most dangerous area. Direct rebounds to the corner, not the slot — or cover the puck if you can't direct it
Your position was wrong on the first shot — you were too deep in your crease so the rebound popped out farther than it should have for a save
You should have frozen the first shot instead of trying to make a clean save and control the puck into your chest for a faceoff draw
The open player passes the puck back across — they send it back to the left half-wall to reset the power play because they don't have a clean shot yet
The open player has a one-timer or clean shot — the PK can't recover across the ice fast enough, so this is a prime scoring chance
The open player skates it to the corner — they carry the puck down low behind the goal line to set up a cycle with the forwards on that side
The play dies — the PK recovers too fast so the open player just holds the puck and waits for everyone to set up in their positions again
You should have pinched harder and finished the check — if you commit to a pinch you have to follow through, so a half pinch is what got beat on the play
You pinched when the puck wasn't clearly yours to win — if you can't get there first, hold the line instead of gambling
Your partner should have covered for you — when one D pinches, the other D rotates to the middle, and your partner didn't read the play in time there
The forward got lucky on the bounce — the puck took a weird bounce off the boards that nobody could have predicted during that battle
Turned faster on the turn — if you had whipped your body around quicker the defender wouldn't have caught up and you'd have kept possession
Turned away from the pressure — to your left — protecting the puck with your body between you and the defender
Shot the puck immediately off the boards — a quick wrist shot toward the net would have been better than trying to turn into a defender
Called for help louder from your teammates — a quick yell brings a winger low to support you along the boards before you get hit
Shoot because you're in the slot — the slot is the best shooting area on the ice, so any puck from there is always worth putting on the net
Pass to the breaking teammate — open player at the back post with goalie unaware is the highest-percentage play
Hold the puck on your stick a second — wait for the defender to commit, then make your read once the play develops in front of you
Skate behind the net first — use the net as a pick on the defender and come out the other side with the puck on your forehand
Freeze the puck immediately to kill the clock — any stoppage you can get late in a one-goal game is good because it burns time off the clock
Leave it for your team — freezing it gives the other team a faceoff in your zone with 90 seconds left, which is dangerous
Kick the puck away from the front of your net — send it anywhere away from the danger area and let your team sort out possession after
Throw the puck to the corner with your glove — get it out of the slot area and let your defensemen go retrieve it behind the goal line
The forwards weren't skating hard enough — if all three had chased with more speed they would have caught the puck before it got passed up
All three forwards went deep — at least one forward needs to stay high to prevent the breakout pass through the middle
The defensemen were too far back at the blue line — they should have stepped up to pinch and kept the puck in the offensive zone right away
The goalie didn't stop the breakout pass across the ice — goalies should play the puck when their team is forechecking hard in the offensive zone
Force a pass to a covered teammate — a quick hard pass can break through the coverage if you put it on the tape before the forechecker reacts
Wheel and carry it out — both forecheckers are committed to your forwards. The D carrying it out is the best option.
Shoot the puck around the boards — rim it hard along the wall past the forecheckers and let your winger chase it up to the neutral zone
Wait behind the net with the puck — hold it until the forecheckers commit to one side and then make your decision based on what they do
Stay in your current low position — you're already set for a shot, so trust your positioning and let the point shooter try to beat you from distance
Move out to the top of your crease immediately — the point shot requires a different position than the low play
Drop into butterfly right away — a point shot usually goes low with traffic in front, so get your pads down early to take away the bottom
Back up to your goal line and square up — giving yourself more time to see the shot is the best play when the puck moves up to the point
The defensemen were out of position when the turnover happened — they were too deep in the offensive zone and couldn't get back in time at all
The forwards didn't transition immediately — when you lose the puck, you turn and skate back right away, no watching
The goalie came out too far from the net — he was too aggressive playing the puck and couldn't recover in time to stop the three-on-two rush
The coach called the wrong play on that shift — the system your team was running left too many forwards deep in the offensive zone area
Goalie holds the puck and stops play for a faceoff — three forecheckers means breakout is too dangerous, so a faceoff is the safer play for your team
Goalie finds the center breaking through the middle or a winger escaping — don't give the puck to a covered player
Goalie shoots the puck hard down the ice — a long clear past their blue line gets the puck out of danger and forces them to reset fully
Goalie rims the puck around the boards behind the net — the winger on the far side can pick it up as it comes around the far corner quick
You were skating too slow to keep up with the play — if you had more speed through the neutral zone you would have caught up to the puck
You entered the zone before the puck offside. You need to time your entry so the puck crosses the line first.
You should have stopped at center ice and waited — a trailing forward should stay back at the red line until the puck is clearly in the zone
The puck carrier was too slow getting over the line — if they had pushed the pace harder you wouldn't have gotten ahead of the puck at all
They dump the puck in and change lines — dumping it deep is the safe play after a turnover so your tired players can change for fresh legs
They attack with speed — the other team is caught deep, so this is a rush chance. Go now before they recover.
They pass the puck back to the goalie to reset — starting a controlled breakout is better than rushing into a broken play with no support up ice
They wait for everyone to get set in position — regrouping at center ice gives your team a full five-player attack into the offensive zone
Play continues normally — the puck came from behind the red line but nobody touched it, so the ref lets the play go and the other team gets possession
The ref blows the whistle for icing — faceoff comes back to your zone and your tired players can't change
Your team gets a power play on the shot — shooting the puck the length of the ice under pressure is legal, and the panic clear gets ruled clean
The other team gets a minor penalty — a defenseman panicking and firing the puck down is a delay of game, so the refs call two minutes on it
Your team backs off and resets — let the other team complete the pass and set up your defensive structure at your own blue line with gap control
Your second forward, who stayed high, intercepts the pass in the slot exactly why they stayed high
Everyone goes to the corner to support — all three forwards collapse low to pin the puck against the boards and win back possession of the play
The play resets at center ice — the blind pass usually gets knocked into neutral ice and both teams skate back to their benches for a new cycle
They should have stayed on the bench longer — too many players out there at once causes confusion, so the fix is a quicker line change on the shift
They left their man open — in the D-zone, everyone needs to have a player covered before chasing the puck
They should have gone straight to the net front — the slot is the most dangerous area in your zone so that's where everyone needs to be
They skated too slow on that play — if they had chased harder they would have won the puck battle and taken possession back for the team
Both D go to the left to cover the two attackers — when the other team overloads one side, your defensemen should both shift there to match numbers
One D takes the two on the left, the other holds the middle and takes the weak-side attacker — both D communicate
Both D shift to the right weak side — defend the back door because that's where the pass is coming to create a tap-in goal for the third attacker
One D goes behind the net to cover — pre-rotate to cover a pass behind the goal line to take away wraparound plays before they develop at all
Spread your legs wide apart in a stance — giving yourself a solid base takes away the low corners of the net and keeps you balanced on the ice
Keep your pads together or slightly overlapping and squeeze your knees — make the 5-hole as small as possible
Drop into a butterfly immediately on the ice — getting your pads flat on the ice seals the 5-hole and takes away any low shot between your legs
Stand with your feet at shoulder width for balance — a normal stance gives you the mobility to react to any shot, including through the 5-hole
Force a pass to a covered teammate — a hard crisp pass through traffic can break coverage if you put it on the tape before a defender reacts
Wheel to the other side of the net — moving forces the defender to move and may open a teammate
Try to power through the defender — use your lower body to drive through the check and carry the puck out in front of the net to shoot
Fall down to draw a penalty call — go to the ice hard and look at the ref so a hooking or holding call gets called on the defender leaning on you
Stop and call loudly for a penalty — the refs will see the stick hold and the push from behind, so let them make the call on the obvious infraction
Keep your feet moving and get your stick free — any shot or swipe on net is worth taking
Fall down hard to draw the penalty — going to the ice with two defenders on you is the easiest way to get the refs to blow the whistle right away
Pass the puck out of the slot area — find a teammate at the point or half-wall to reset the play since you're tied up by two defenders
Give up on the shot — you can't see it — if there's no way to track the puck just cover the low corners and hope it hits you for a lucky save
Move laterally to find a gap around the screener, call to your D to move them, and be ready for a tip
Drop into butterfly early to cover the bottom half — most screened shots go low because the shooter can't see either, so seal the ice completely
Stay completely still and trust positioning — any movement takes you out of the angle, so hold your spot and let the puck hit you on the save
Nothing — just random body language — shooters move their arms in all kinds of ways before a shot and there's no real pattern you can read off it
They're likely shooting high — a dropping elbow often indicates a high release
They're going to pass instead — a dropping elbow usually means the shooter is about to sauce a pass across to a teammate on the weak side
They'll deke first before shooting — dropping the elbow is how shooters load up for a deke move to the forehand before they put it in the net
You pass the puck anyway
You stop skating right away
You take the shot — the defender just opened the lane for you by leaving
You dump the puck in deep
You keep skating backward to the outside and let them cut — stay in your lane and trust your positioning, since forcing outside is still the goal
You pivot and match their cut — stay on their inside hip and use your body to close them off
You stop and watch the cut — let them cross the middle so you can see where they're going before you commit to a defensive move
You go for a big hip check — when they cut back inside they expose themselves to a check, so step up and knock them off the puck quick
Chase the puck carrier at top speed into corner — full pressure on the puck forces a bad pass and keeps the offensive zone pressure alive for your team
Read their breakout pass options and cut them off — be in the lane before the pass is
Give up on the play and change lines — you're already tired from the forecheck, so get to the bench and let fresh legs take over the shift
Back off to the blue line and reset — let them break out clean and set up your neutral zone defense to stop them at the red line with the D
Yes — pinch hard and help — crash down on the puck before the defender can control it because pinching saves an offensive zone shift
No — if possession is about to be lost, pinching puts you deep with no coverage during
Go to the net front instead — crash the crease area and look for a rebound while your forward battles for the puck along the boards there
Take a quick shot from the line as a distraction — firing anything on net gets everyone looking there so your forward can win the puck clean
Go into the corner and chase the puck hard — win the battle with numbers by joining your teammate so you can pin the puck along the boards
Stay high between the two defensemen — cut off passes and take away the middle
Go straight to the net front — be a screen and look for rebounds while the low forwards battle for the puck along the boards in the corner
Back up to the blue line and wait — let your low forwards work the corner and be ready to support if the puck comes to you up top there
Yes — go help your winger immediately — crash down fast and support the battle because two players on the puck wins more than one every time
No — hold the blue line. If your winger loses the puck you'll be caught deep.
Go to the net front — crash the crease for a rebound while your winger battles along the boards and let your partner cover the point area
Skate behind the net to support — work down low and be ready to receive a D-to-D pass along the goal line from your winning teammate
Slow down and let them set up their defense — forcing the play into two pinching D is risky, so wait for your team to get on side behind you
Skate hard behind them — you have a breakaway if you beat them to their blue line
Dump the puck in deep — chip it past both D and chase because they're pinching up too high to recover on a dump-in behind them
Pass across to a winger on the far side — switch the play to the weak side where there's more open ice to attack the zone together
Pass the puck down to the corner — find a teammate low behind the goal line to start a cycle with the defender cheating up toward the net instead
Shoot from the half-wall — you have time and the slot defender is distracted
Pass up to the point defender — move the puck to the blue line for a one-timer since you have time and the slot defender isn't watching you
Drop the puck behind you immediately — leave it for a trailing teammate to pick up since nobody is pressuring you on the half-wall right now
Ignore the D and pass to the corner — keep the cycle going low instead of feeding a defender who just stepped down from the point to the slot
Pass to the D who stepped in — they now have a shot from a dangerous area
Shoot from the corner yourself — a bad-angle shot can still create a rebound for a teammate and the D stepping in acts as a screen now
Pass up the boards to a winger — find a teammate along the wall to reset the play because the D stepping in leaves the point wide open
Just play hard until someone tells you otherwise — effort wins games, so skate as hard as you can and the right plays will happen on their own out there
Know your specific job for the next 30 seconds — faceoff role, where to go, who to cover
Try to score the first time you touch the puck no matter what — you're down by one with 30 seconds left so every shot needs to go on net now
Get pumped up and skate as fast as you can — energy wins the faceoff and the battles, so get loud on the bench and bring it on the next shift
Commit to the puck carrier on your left immediately — step up hard and take away the shot because the puck carrier is the biggest threat on the rush
Back up fast enough to not get beaten by their speed, stay in the middle, and force the shooter
Race forward toward the net — get back to your crease to help your goalie on the shot coming from the puck carrier on the left side of the ice
Skate to the weak side and cover the off-wing attacker — take away the back door pass and let your goalie handle the shot from the puck carrier on left
Always wait for the perfect shot after a catch — take an extra second to settle the puck and pick your spot before releasing the shot on the goalie
Released immediately on the catch — the opening was there when you first received it
Passed to an open teammate in the slot — don't shoot unless you have a perfect look, and moving the puck one more time opens a better chance
Skated in closer to the net first — get to the top of the crease where the goalie can't cover as much net before you release the shot up high
Commit to the puck carrier on the left — step up hard and take away the shot because stopping the puck carrier removes the biggest threat on the rush
Stay in the middle — take away the pass to the cutter and force the shot. Your goalie handles the shot.
Chase the cutter to the right — take away the pass and the tip since the cutter is the biggest threat and your goalie can stop the shot from the puck carrier on the left
Back up all the way to your crease — give your goalie the best sight line and let them handle the two-on-one from the top of the crease area
Set up the power play again and let your teammates handle everything — your PP is already working so you reset in the offensive zone and run it again
Get the puck to a breaking teammate immediately — the 2-on-2 counter is better than resetting the PP
Hold the puck along the boards and regroup — let your power play catch up and set up the attack after you've re-entered the offensive zone clean
Dump the puck in deep and chase — get it behind the defense and forecheck hard since you've got fresh legs on your power play unit already
Keep skating because you can't help anymore — get back to the net and let your goalie handle the slap shot since you're out of position now anyway
Get your stick in the shooting lane to deflect or block — you're close enough
Dive
Call for your D to rush the shooter — yell to your defenseman to step up from the middle and challenge the shooter before the slap shot gets off
Stay tight on the post and trust it — the post takes away the short side so don't move even if the shooter cuts across the crease area on you
Push off the post and move with them — they've changed the angle and you need to stay square to the puck
Drop into a butterfly on the post — seal the ice along the post and take away the low shot since they're cutting toward the middle from the goal line there
Come way out into the crease to challenge — step out aggressively to cut down the angle as they cut across the front of the net to shoot
Stay on the right post and trust your teammates — your D should take the backdoor player so hold your post and take away the half-wall shooter
T-push hard across to the left post — the backdoor player is the immediate danger, the half-wall shooter is not
Come out of your crease toward the puck carrier on the half-wall — challenge the shooter and take away the angle since they have the puck right now
Drop into a butterfly on the right post — seal the ice on the short side and let your defenseman take the backdoor player cutting to the far post
